channel.title: The Grant Desk - next 90 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: next 90 days.

item.title: Club Nothing: Community Music Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Nothing

Location: Anywhere in the world.

Award: USD 40,000 fund total; four individuals or collectives each receive USD 10,000 to produce a club event, series, or festival. No application fee.

Duration: Event, series, or festival to take place later in 2026; winners announced late August 2026.

Deadline: 09 Aug 2026

Club Nothing is a global events series and USD 40,000 fund from the tech brand Nothing, supporting organisers, artists, and collectives creating new music experiences. The fund will award four individuals or collectives USD 10,000 each to throw a club event, series, or festival later this year, backing disruptors bringing new sounds and experiences to their communities through nightlife anywhere in the world. Applications are judged by a panel including DJ and producer Manuka Honey (Marissa Malik), Nothing Global Senior Community Manager and Madruga Festival co-founder Erica Travis, Nothing Global Head of Communications Shavone Charles, Dazed Music Editor Solomon Pace-McCarrick, A&R and music-publishing founder James Supreme, and music-industry professional Layla Mustafa Rock. Applications close 9 August 2026; winners announced late August 2026. Learn more and apply at nothing.tech/club-nothing (details also posted at https://nothing.community/d/59214-want-10000-to-throw-a-party). See full terms at nothing.tech/club-nothing/terms-and-conditions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Call to Artists: Walden Fire Station and Library (Calgary Arts Development)
item.description:
Organisation: Calgary Arts Development (CADA)

Location: Walden community, Calgary, Alberta, Canada. Open to local and national (Canadian) professional artists/artist teams.

Award: Total project budget of CAD 275,000 (exclusive of all applicable taxes) for one selected artist/artist team. The artist fee for the total project is CAD 68,750 + GST. Budget breakdown can be adapted to the artist's approach, following CARFAC's recommendation that a minimum 20% of the budget go to the artist's fee; the total allocated budget will not be increased.

Duration: Concept and design from September 2026 to end of July 2027; fabrication and installation (Phase 2) from the second half of 2027 into 2028.

Deadline: 28 Jul 2026

Calgary Arts Development (CADA) is seeking an artist or artist team to join The City of Calgary's Walden Fire Station and Library project team to create permanent public artwork(s) for a combined fire station and library planned for the community of Walden. One artist/artist team will be selected with a total project budget of CAD 275,000 (exclusive of all applicable taxes); the opportunity is open to local and national professional artists/artist teams working in various media. ELIGIBILITY: local and national mid-career to late-career professional public artists and artist teams with a minimum of five years' previous experience creating artwork in the public realm; they must have at least three outdoor permanent public art installations, each with a lifespan of over 10 years and a minimum project budget of CAD 150,000 (see the Canada Council for the Arts definition of a professional artist). Walden does not currently have an active community association, so the project requires experience in artist-led community engagement and a strong understanding of alternative outreach. Artists must be available to meet the project timelines from September 2026 to the end of concept and design by July 2027; fabrication and installation take place in Phase 2, starting in the second half of 2027 and continuing into 2028. REWARD: total project budget CAD 275,000 (artist fee CAD 68,750 + GST); CADA is open to adapting the budget breakdown to the artist's approach, following CARFAC's recommendation of a minimum 20% to the artist's fee, though the total budget will not be increased. TIMELINE: applications open 16 June 2026 (9am MT); pre-recorded information session posted early July; application deadline 28 July 2026 (no applications accepted after 1pm MT); shortlisted interviews mid-September; results late September 2026. Eligibility questions: walden@calgaryartsdevelopment.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Genesis Mission AI Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service (with Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science)

Location: United States. Fellows are forward deployed to US Department of Energy national labs (initial partners: Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory). Remote or in-person; on-site travel at least once a quarter. Must be eligible to work in the US (no visa sponsorship; US green card holders eligible).

Award: Prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000.

Duration: 9-12 months, starting fall 2026.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Horizon Institute for Public Service, Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science are partnering to launch a fellowship that embeds technical AI talent across the US Department of Energy national labs to advance the goals of the Genesis Mission, DOE's flagship science and technology initiative launched in late 2025. The Genesis Mission identifies 26 national science and technology challenges critical to US leadership and envisions a national platform connecting supercomputers, experimental facilities, AI systems, and unique datasets, with DOE's 17 national labs playing a key role. Genesis Mission AI Fellows are forward deployed to DOE national labs to bring frontier AI expertise to high-priority scientific challenges. As core members of the technical staff on a Genesis Mission project, fellows work alongside national lab scientists and partners and apply AI to national priorities, from building cutting-edge models of plasma dynamics in pursuit of controlled fusion to advancing state-of-the-art models for foundational materials discovery. Initial national lab partners include Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory; additional partners may be announced later. COMPENSATION/DURATION: 9-12 months starting fall 2026, with a prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000; fellows are expected to travel to their national lab site at least once a quarter but may work on-site if they prefer. ELIGIBILITY: competitive applicants should have at least 2 years of industry experience building AI models or agents (those with industry experience building AI applications or data infrastructure will also be considered); scientific domain expertise is not required, but any placement will involve some training and study in a specific scientific discipline or energy topic, supported by expert colleagues at the host institution. All fellows must be eligible to work in the United States for the duration of the placement; US employment visas cannot be sponsored, but US permanent residents (green card holders) are eligible. APPLICATION: initial form requires a CV or resume (max 2 pages) and short-answer questions on motivations and experiences (max 200 words); successful applicants complete interviews through partner organizations and national labs, and selected fellows are matched to partner labs by scientific domain and availability. The review uses evidence-based best practices including blind grading of application materials. TIMELINE: application deadline 31 July 2026; final fellow selection August-September 2026; expected start fall 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency 2026-2027
item.description:
Organisation: Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU), Art + Design Department

Location: Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center, Northeastern Illinois University, Chicago, Illinois, United States. In-person; semester-long access to NEIU facilities.

Award: USD 800 honorarium, plus a semester of full access to NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to the project (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture); supplies can be requested with guidance and approval from Studio Area personnel. Visiting Artists may also exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery.

Duration: One semester. Residency Session 1: 25 January - 14 May; Session 2: 30 August - 11 December.

Deadline: 09 Aug 2026

The NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency strives to create visible connections between Northeastern Illinois University's diverse population of art and design students and working artists who embody these identities, providing insights and inspiration through a dynamic exchange of ideas and techniques. Visiting Artists should demonstrate a commitment to their artistic practice and an interest in engaging with NEIU's students and creative community. Each Visiting Artist is awarded an honorarium and has full access for a semester to the NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to their project; over the residency they showcase their work in a public academic forum, lead a hands-on workshop, and participate in a Portfolio Review Day for NEIU's Art + Design students. FACILITIES (in the Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center) include a Design Studio with the largest collection of Riso drums at any Chicago university, a Fab Lab (FDM and resin 3D printers, large-format CNC router, vinyl cutters, laser cutters), a Ceramic Studio (throwing wheels, glaze room, electric and gas kilns, raku area), a Letterpress Studio (two Vandercook presses, metal and wood type), a Sculpture Studio (wood shop, clay modeling and spray painting areas), a Photography Studio (analog and digital, plus a lighting studio), and fully equipped Painting, Drawing, and Printmaking studios. BENEFITS: USD 800 honorarium; facility access based on project focus (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture), with supplies requestable subject to approval; and the opportunity to exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery. PARTICIPATION: lead a 2-hour hands-on workshop for 15-20 students on a topic relevant to the project; give an hour-long artist talk with Q&A; and serve as a guest panelist for a day-long portfolio review. KEY DATES: open call 2 June - 9 August; emailed acceptances in October; Residency Session 1 runs 25 January - 14 May and Session 2 runs 30 August - 11 December. Apply via the JotForm application. Deadline 9 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Digital Democracy Centre x TrygFonden Fellowship Program 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Digital Democracy Centre (University of Southern Denmark), with support from TrygFonden

Location: Odense, Denmark (University of Southern Denmark). Flexible mix of in-office stays at the DDC in Odense and remote periods; stays planned to the Danish working calendar.

Award: Either a salary subsidy or a stipend to help with travel and/or housing; specific benefits (financial support, access to DDC research resources, flexible working spaces, speaker series, networks) vary by agreement between the fellow, DDC, and partnering organizations. Applicants describe the posts they seek support for (flights, housing, research costs, salary compensation, stipend, etc.).

Duration: Flexible; typically 2-6 months (minimum two months, can include shorter DDC stays and remote periods), longer in exceptional cases. Fellowships start anywhere in 2027.

Deadline: 01 Sep 2026

The Digital Democracy Centre (DDC) at the University of Southern Denmark, with support from TrygFonden, is calling for applications for its 2027 fellowship cohort. The program fosters collaboration between researchers, journalists, and policymakers exploring the impact of digital technology and AI on media, politics, and democracy. Fellows collaborate with a self-selected DDC mentor to generate new knowledge, contribute to public debate, develop networks, and produce tangible outreach outputs such as policy briefs, educational packages, and talks. THREE FELLOWSHIP TYPES: (1) academic fellows researching digital technologies and democracy; (2) journalist fellows covering digital technology and democracy; (3) policy fellows contributing to the regulatory debate. Target group: researchers, journalism graduates, and policymakers in research/educational institutions, media organizations, ministries, NGOs, etc. PRIORITIES: projects with clear relevance to Denmark or Danish society (including comparative/international projects yielding insights relevant to a Danish context); in 2027, projects related to DDC's Hybrid Warfare Initiative (societal resilience to new forms of international conflict, with a focus on digital technologies and AI) are also prioritized. EXPECTATIONS: applicants must secure confirmation of mentorship from their desired DDC mentor BEFORE applying (applications without a confirmed mentor are automatically rejected; PhD students and research assistants are generally not eligible as mentors, and Sten Schaumburg-Muller is unavailable as a mentor for the 2027 call); applicants must also coordinate support from their current supervisors/managers. Fellows engage in DDC activities, seminars/workshops, and public outreach, and submit a short report within two months of finishing. APPLICATION (in English): preliminary project title, fellowship type, brief project description (max 1000 chars), detailed description and timeline (max 2400 chars, including duration, confirmed mentor, and remote/in-office work plan), description of activities and collaboration with external practice partners (max 1000 chars), expected product/impact/output (max 500 chars), a CV (max 5 pages), and an overview of the posts for which financial support is sought. Submit the application form (PDF or Word) plus CV as PDFs to ddc@sdu.dk with subject line 'DDC / TrygFonden Fellowship application'. Deadline 1 September 2026; decisions communicated no later than 1 October 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: 100 Voices of Florida Film Grant
item.description:
Organisation: 100 Voices of Florida

Location: Florida, United States

Award: Up to USD 5,000 (unrestricted) USD 25 application fee with a Florida connection, USD 35 without

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: 20 Sep 2026

100 Voices of Florida is a grant for filmmakers and screenwriters that awards up to USD 5,000 in unrestricted funds, which winners can use for finishing funds, festival submission fees, cast and crew reimbursements, production, or anything else. Applicants submit either a short film (under twenty minutes) or a script (twenty pages or less); films in other languages must include accurate English subtitles, and scripts must be in English. The grant is open to writers, directors, and producers who are at least 18 years old, and creative teams may submit, though only one entrant receives the award. A panel of judges with professional filmmaking experience evaluates entries, and the winner also receives a meeting with an industry professional. The deadline for the current cycle is September 20, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Hayama Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Hayama Artist Residency

Location: Hayama, Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan

Award: Fully funded: roundtrip airfare to Tokyo (Narita or Haneda), shared accommodation for 4 weeks, and USD 800 for meals and local transportation. Group exhibition at KOKI ARTS gallery in Tokyo. USD 95 application fee for first-time applicants

Duration: 4 weeks

Deadline: 30 Sep 2026

The Hayama Artist Residency introduces visual artists from around the world to Japanese culture and offers them their first gallery exhibition in Japan. The four-week residency takes place in Hayama, a coastal town in Kanagawa Prefecture about 1.5 hours from Tokyo. Selected artists receive roundtrip airfare to Tokyo, shared accommodation with one other resident, and USD 800 for meals and local transportation. A core element is a group exhibition during the stay, hosted in partnership with KOKI ARTS, a contemporary gallery in the Bakurocho art district of Tokyo. The program is open to visual artists over 21 working in any medium, based anywhere in the world, and applications are reviewed by the director and an international selection committee. The June 2027 cycle has an application deadline of September 30, 2026, with notification by December 15, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Eamonn McCabe Bursary 2026 (Royal Photographic Society)
item.description:
Organisation: The Royal Photographic Society (RPS), in partnership with Eamonn McCabe's family and The Guardian

Location: International bursary. Applicants must be aged 30 years or under on the closing date. Interviews held via Zoom.

Award: GBP 3,000 to deliver a photography project on the theme of 'sporting endeavour'. The completed project will be published by The Guardian and the RPS, who will work with the recipient to bring the project to a wider audience.

Duration: The project should be completed within one year of being awarded.

Deadline: 03 Aug 2026

The Eamonn McCabe Bursary, launched in 2024, commemorates the life of photojournalist Eamonn McCabe (1948-2022), who made his name as a sports photographer before moving to wider editorial work, mainly for The Observer and The Guardian, and who supported a generation of photographers from the 1980s to the 2020s. Run by the RPS in partnership with McCabe's family and The Guardian, this international bursary continues that legacy. AWARD: GBP 3,000 to a photographer aged 30 years or under to deliver a photography project responding to the theme of 'sporting endeavour', which can be interpreted as widely as the applicant wishes, though the general approach should be suitable for publication. The project should normally be new, not previously exhibited or published. As well as professional sports, the call particularly encourages projects covering grassroots and community activities (for example, addressing issues such as mental health or supporting displaced communities), and welcomes creative interpretations of the theme. The completed project will be published by The Guardian and the RPS. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be aged 30 years or under on the closing date. PROCESS: interviews via Zoom in September; recipient announced by the end of September; project completed within one year of award. Apply via the RPS application portal using RPS Account credentials. Closing date 3 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Creative Steps for Individuals 2026 (Arts Council of Wales)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Wales

Location: Wales, United Kingdom. For artists and creatives based in Wales.

Award: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs). Applicants can apply for up to 90% of the developmental cost; the remaining 10% must come from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both).

Duration: Supports a stage of professional development; allow a minimum of 10 working weeks between the deadline and the project start date.

Deadline: 29 Jul 2026

Creative Steps funds the professional, business and organisational development of artists who have experienced barriers to careers within the arts. Creative Steps for Individuals is open to individual artists and creatives who identify as being Ethnically and Culturally diverse, D/deaf, disabled or neurodivergent, groups that are underrepresented in Arts Council of Wales funding programmes due to barriers to entry. Applicants must have demonstrable experience as an artist or creative professional; those without may still be supported on the basis of artistic potential by submitting a portfolio, CV or examples of work, discussed with a Relationship Manager. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: career development, NOT creating new work or project delivery. Examples include peer-to-peer learning, training courses (not formal/vocational education), building networks, mentorship/coaching, business development/social media/marketing advice, website development/marketing materials/equipment or materials, and setting up a new organisation. Budgets may include the applicant's own time, freelancer costs (paid at standard industry rates), mentor/consultant costs, access costs, and childcare/caregiving costs. AMOUNT: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs); up to 90% of the developmental cost, with the remaining 10% from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both, confirmed by letters of support). No application fee. APPLY: applicants must express interest at least 3 weeks before the deadline to receive support from an Application Coach or Access Support coordinator; an application form is provided by a Relationship Manager. The fund runs in rounds; the next round (Round 2 2026) has an Expression of Interest cut-off of 8 July 2026 and an application deadline of 29 July 2026. Later rounds: Round 3 (EOI 30 September, deadline 21 October 2026) and Round 4 (EOI 10 February, deadline 3 March 2027).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SIAP: Project Funding for Individual Artists 2026 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.

Award: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Project-based activity; duration not specified.

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

Project Funding for Individuals, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds project-based creative activities across all artistic disciplines. Eligible activities include creating new work, research and development, residencies, mentoring, professional development, and time dedicated to specific creative outcomes. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, artist fees/time, residency costs, travel (mileage at GBP 0.257/mile), venue hire, technical equipment (max 50% of request value), and IT equipment (laptops/PCs up to GBP 1,200; tablets up to GBP 500). ELIGIBILITY: artists of all disciplines and career stages, including emerging artists (less than 5 years professional experience), and in some cases technical staff/administrators; students and academics at third-level institutions may apply with departmental approval showing the costs are not covered by their employer or academic work. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon (online submission required).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SIAP: Artists' International Development Fund 2026 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland; international development takes place abroad with overseas partners/hosts.

Award: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Early-stage international development opportunities; residencies supported for a maximum of 4 weeks.

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

The Artists' International Development Fund (AIDF), part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds early-stage international development opportunities for individual, freelance and self-employed artists to build networks abroad. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, travel, residencies (up to 4 weeks), technical fees, venue hire, and equipment, with IT devices capped at GBP 1,200 for laptops/PCs and GBP 500 for tablets. ELIGIBILITY: artists across all disciplines, particularly emerging artists (under 5 years professional experience); academics and students at third-level institutions may apply if the project differs from their employment/studies. AIDF applicants must have received recognition for their work in Northern Ireland, lack extensive international experience, and demonstrate interest in their work from an overseas partner/host. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEU Commission 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Page Not Found

Location: The Netherlands; open to non-EEA-national students enrolled at Dutch art academies.

Award: EUR 2,000 each to three commissioned writers, paid as an advance in September 2026.

Duration: One-time commission; commissioned texts published at a later date

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Page Not Found announces the third edition of the NEU Commission, which intends to support the emergence of alternative voices within contemporary art. It targets a group currently under-represented in Europe: art students with limited financial means who are required to provide a guarantee for their visa and to pay high non-EU tuition fees, economical barriers to their participation in art and knowledge production in The Netherlands. Page Not Found will commission three art students from non-European countries to each write an essay or a free-form text in English. Each selected writer receives EUR 2,000 for the commission, provided as an advance payment in September 2026; the commissioned texts are published at a later date. OPEN CALL: only students enrolled in Dutch art academies who are NOT nationals of the European Economic Area are eligible. The commissioned text does not need to be submitted when answering the open call; the jury selects recipients based on their previous writing and financial need. Review the Rules of the Open Call and the FAQ before applying. Deadline: Friday 31 July 2026, 23:59 CEST (Amsterdam).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Prisma Art Prize 20th Edition
item.description:
Organisation: Prisma Art Prize

Location: Rome, Italy

Award: EUR 2,000 annual cash fund distributed across quarterly winners, plus group exhibition at Palazzo Merulana, inclusion in the collective catalogue, artist interviews, and eligibility for solo show prizes (Isorrophia Gallery, Pallavicini-Dettori) and partner art residencies (Dar Meso, Casacon, Osservatoriomaree) EUR 34 entry fee for 1 to 3 artworks, plus EUR 5 for each additional artwork up to a maximum of 8

Duration: Group exhibition runs November 5 to 8, 2026

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

The Prisma Art Prize is an international award for painting and two-dimensional artistic research based in Rome. The 20th edition selects emerging and established artists working in painting, drawing, illustration, printmaking, and traditional mixed media for a group exhibition at Palazzo Merulana, opening November 5, 2026. Winners share an annual cash fund of EUR 2,000 distributed across quarterly calls and receive inclusion in the collective catalogue plus artist interviews. Additional opportunities include solo exhibition prizes and fully funded placements at partner art residencies. The prize is open internationally to all living artists regardless of age, gender, nationality, or educational background. The submission deadline for this edition is September 10, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Dedalus Foundation Senior Fellowship 2027
item.description:
Organisation: The Dedalus Foundation

Location: United States (remote, project-based)

Award: Stipend up to USD 30,000

Duration: Up to one year

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Dedalus Foundation, established by artist Robert Motherwell, awards a Senior Fellowship supporting critical and historical studies related to painting, sculpture, and allied arts of the twentieth century. The fellowship is open to art historians, critics, curators, and other writers and scholars who have demonstrated their abilities through previous accomplishments. Awards provide a stipend of up to USD 30,000 for projects lasting up to one year, with the amount varying by project needs. Applicants must be U.S. citizens and may not be currently pursuing academic degrees. Applications are submitted online and reference letters through a separate form. A distinguished scholar jury selects recipients, with awards announced by mid-December.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Jentel Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Jentel Foundation

Location: Banner, Wyoming, USA

Award: USD 100 weekly stipend (about USD 300 total over three weeks), plus private bedroom, separate studio space, and shared living and kitchen facilities provided at no cost USD 30 application fee

Duration: Approximately 3 weeks (arrive on the 15th, depart the morning of the 7th of the following month)

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Jentel Artist Residency offers approximately three-week residencies in the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains near Banner, Wyoming, giving artists, writers, and composers dedicated time for creative work. Sessions run from mid-January through early December, hosting six residents at a time. It is open to visual artists in any medium, writers of fiction, creative nonfiction, or poetry, and composers across musical genres. Residents receive a private bedroom, a separate studio, shared living and kitchen space, and a USD 100 weekly stipend to help cover food and personal expenses. Applicants must be at least 25 years old and be U.S. citizens or foreign nationals whose primary residence is in the U.S. during the application year, and may not be enrolled full-time in a degree program. There is a USD 30 non-refundable application fee, with deadlines of September 15 for Winter and Spring sessions and January 15 for Summer and Fall sessions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Textile Exchange x Atmos: Fashion Regenerated 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Textile Exchange (with Atmos)

Location: Open call; work to be promoted globally through Textile Exchange and Atmos and exhibited at the 2026 Textile Exchange conference.

Award: Overall winner: a paid collaborative commission worth USD 10,000 with Textile Exchange to develop a new body of work documenting the evidence of regeneration, published through Atmos and Textile Exchange and shown at the 2026 conference. Two runners-up: work promoted by Textile Exchange and displayed at the conference exhibition, plus a USD 2,000 cash prize.

Duration: One-off commission/prize.

Deadline: 20 Jul 2026

Fashion Regenerated is an open call from Textile Exchange and Atmos. Winners are selected by a panel of expert judges and, for the first time, a public vote. The shortlist is promoted through Textile Exchange and Atmos, amplifying inspirational stories to an engaged audience of thousands. The overall winner receives a paid collaborative commission to the value of USD 10,000 with Textile Exchange to develop a new body of work documenting the evidence of regeneration, working closely with the team to identify the right story and access; the winning work is published through Atmos, Textile Exchange, and shown to an audience of more than 1,300 changemakers at the 2026 Textile Exchange conference. Two runners-up have their work promoted by Textile Exchange and displayed at the conference exhibition, and there is also a USD 2,000 cash prize. Check the call site for full eligibility and submission details. Deadline: 20 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SIAP: Major Individual Artists Award 2026/27 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.

Award: Up to GBP 15,000 per award; four awards available in 2026/27. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Supports specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development and materials/equipment. Activity duration not specified.

Deadline: 20 Jul 2026

The Major Individual Artists award, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds extended or ambitious work by established artists, covering specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development, and materials/equipment. AMOUNT: four awards of up to GBP 15,000 each in 2026/27. ELIGIBILITY: established artists across all disciplines with national/international recognition who have not previously received a major award; academics and postgraduate artists are eligible if the project costs do not relate to their institutional work or studies. Applicants must contact the relevant artform officer before submission (by 10 July 2026); applications without this prior contact are ineligible. No application fee. The online application system closes precisely at the deadline and applicants must receive a confirmation email to verify successful submission. Deadline: Monday 20 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Illuminations Grant for Black Trans Women Visual Artists
item.description:
Organisation: Queer|Art

Location: United States; open to self-identified Black trans women and trans femmes working in visual art and based in the US.

Award: USD 10,000 to one winning artist, plus USD 1,250 each to four distinguished finalists. Winners and finalists also receive additional professional development resources and guidance, and each judge conducts a studio visit with the winning artist.

Duration: One-time award

Deadline: 02 Jul 2026

Now in its 7th year, the Illuminations Grant for Black Trans Women Visual Artists is an annual USD 10,000 grant from Queer|Art that provides critical support to Black trans women whose work has often been under-recognized in the visual art field. To further recognize finalists for their artistic achievements, Queer|Art also provides a USD 1,250 award to four distinguished finalists. Winning artists and finalists receive additional professional development resources and further guidance to bolster their creative development. The grant was developed and named in partnership with Mariette Pathy Allen, Aaryn Lang, and Serena Jara, and is made possible entirely through support from visual artist Mariette Pathy Allen, whose photographic work over the last forty years has focused on expanding cultural consciousness around gender and transformation. It is administered through Queer|Art with a rotating panel of judges, each of whom conducts a studio visit with the winning artist as part of the award's focus on creative and professional development; judges for the 2026 cycle include Jordyn Jay, Isis Awad, and Utē Petit. ELIGIBILITY: qualified artists must be self-identified Black trans women and trans femmes working in visual art and based in the United States. Deadline: July 2, 2026.

item.title: Feminist Gaming Fellowship: Open Call 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Feminist Gaming Project

Location: Remote across the Netherlands, Türkiye, Germany, and the UK

Award: EUR 1,000 honorarium per fellow (paid half at the start, half on completion)

Duration: Three months, August to October 2026; minimum ~6 hours per week (~75 hours total)

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

The Feminist Gaming Project is opening applications for its first Fellowship cohort: five fellows from different creative and research fields spending three months developing 3D assets, tools, and documentation for its Open Access Asset Library (OAL), a free, open repository of inclusive 3D content created by, with, and for intersectionally feminist and diverse communities. Where mainstream marketplaces default to hyper-athletic, idealized, or hyper-sexualized characters, the OAL offers average bodies, real postures, everyday objects, and the kinds of lives most games leave out (pregnancy and postpartum, care work, ageing, disability, domestic labor). This first cohort is dedicated to 3D content. Supported by the Creative Industries Fund NL and working across the Netherlands, Türkiye, Germany, and the UK, the project challenges exclusionary norms in how games are made. The fellowship offers a EUR 1,000 honorarium per fellow over three months (half at start, half on completion); mentorship from the core team and advisors in feminist design, game studies, 3D production, and digital culture; a peer community across NL, TR, DE, and the UK with biweekly meetings; visibility through a travelling exhibition program, publications, and public events; and authorship credited in the OAL under its license terms. Five roles are recruited (one fellow each): 3D Character Artist for inclusive base meshes; 3D Character Artist for pregnancy and postpartum assets; 3D Animator for neutral and everyday movement; 3D Modeller for domestic and care-labor environments; and Researcher/Writer for documentation and the representation framework; applicants may rank up to two. Open to 3D artists, animators, modellers, designers, researchers, and writers committed to inclusive and intersectional representation in games; prior game-industry experience is not required, and emerging creators, underrepresented practitioners, and researchers at the intersection of games, gender, and culture are especially encouraged, particularly those based in or connected to NL, TR, DE, or the UK. To apply, email feministgamingproject@gmail.com with a short statement (max ~500 words), a portfolio or work samples (a link is fine), and confirmation of availability for the three-month period; name the role(s) in the subject line or statement. The project explicitly asks applicants to write statements in their own voice rather than using AI-generated text. Indicative timeline: applications close 12 July 2026; selection and notification late July; fellowship period August to October 2026; showcase and publication November to December 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

Loghaven Artist Residency offers practicing artists time, space, and resources on ninety wooded acres in Knoxville, Tennessee. Organisation: The Center for Fiction

Location: New York City, USA

Award: USD 5,000 grant plus writers studio space, mentorship, agent introductions, and community programming

Duration: One year (the fellowship year)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship annually supports nine early-career, New York City-based fiction writers at a pivotal point in their careers. Each fellow receives a 5,000 USD grant, dedicated space and time to write in the Writers Studio, personalized editorial mentorship, opportunities to meet literary agents, and community programming. item.title: MSB Interpretive Panels: Public Art Commission (Creative Philadelphia)
item.description:
Organisation: Creative Philadelphia (Office of Arts, Culture and the Creative Economy)

Location: Municipal Services Building, Center City, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States. Philadelphia-based artists strongly encouraged; artists from outside the region considered. Multiple in-person site visits required (travel costs covered within the project budget).

Award: Total project budget of USD 250,000, which must cover: artist fees (18% design fee), design and engineering, fabrication and installation, structural footings/anchoring, travel and site visits, AR development (software, hosting, content production), insurance (1%), community engagement participation, and a service warranty keeping content compatible with new mobile OS versions for a minimum of 10 years. No application fees.

Duration: Permanent public artwork commission; two-part selection process.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Creative Philadelphia announces a call to commission a series of permanent interpretive panels with integrated augmented reality (AR) components at the Municipal Services Building in Center City Philadelphia. The commission is part of a broader initiative that includes an upcoming public sculpture of Dr. Sadie T.M. Alexander by artist Vinnie Bagwell. Inspired by Alexander's groundbreaking legacy in public service, these interpretive panels will celebrate the City of Philadelphia's municipal employees (past, present, and future) and provide deeper context for understanding Dr. Alexander's impact; they will be located in front of the building's landscaped areas, flanking the upcoming sculpture, and are intended to complement and support the overall site experience. SCOPE: an artist team will design and fabricate four (4) permanent, sculptural interpretive panels incorporating augmented reality (AR) elements to expand storytelling beyond the physical surface of the artwork. ELIGIBILITY: all interested artists are invited to apply; Philadelphia-based artists are strongly encouraged, though artists from outside the region will be considered; multiple in-person site visits are required and associated travel costs must be covered within the project budget. SELECTION: a two-part process: Part 1 submission of qualifications; Part 2 finalist proposals and interviews. Up to three (3) finalists are invited to submit short concept proposals and attend an interview; at no point should artists submit design proposals. BUDGET: USD 250,000 total (see amount for the required cost breakdown). An Artist Informational Meeting is held via Zoom on 30 June, 6:00-7:00pm. Application questions: paige.phillips@phila.gov. Apply via Submittable. Deadline 12 July 2026 (submissions close Monday 13 July 2026, 6:00 AM).

Open call details

item.title: GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (hybrid; remote participation available)

Award: Free to participate; in-person attendees provided dinner (value kept at or below USD 20 per session)

Duration: 12-week part-time program, early September to early December 2026 (approximately 5 hours per week)

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

The GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program is a bipartisan, part-time, 12-week program designed to accelerate or launch careers in U.S. AI policy. Open to U.S. policy professionals across government and think tanks, it builds a technically-informed understanding of the AI policy landscape through guided discussion sessions, with an emphasis on general-purpose AI. Topics include how general-purpose AI systems are developed; inputs to AI development such as energy, chips, and data centers; AI's effects on productivity, scientific discovery, and economic growth; national security and great power competition; corporate self-governance; AI regulation in other jurisdictions; and federal and state AI regulatory proposals in the U.S. Participants also have weekly opportunities to question world-leading experts; prior guest speakers have included White House OSTP staffers, former frontier-lab employees, and people who helped draft U.S. state- and federal-level AI policy proposals. The program runs early September to early December 2026 (break for Thanksgiving week) with a roughly 5-hour weekly commitment: two hours of synchronous evening sessions (EST), held in-person at GovAI's D.C. office near DuPont Circle or remotely with cameras on, plus three hours of independent reading and podcasts from a free syllabus. To list completion on a resume, participants must attend 9 of 12 sessions, write a short policy memo or op-ed, and pass an interview-based exam. Eligible participants include executive and legislative branch staff (fellows, interns, detailees, military personnel, national laboratory personnel) and think-tank policy researchers and affiliates, across a range of seniority levels, with preference for the D.C. metropolitan area; participants need not currently cover AI but should aspire to a career in U.S. tech or national security policy. Selection has two stages: an online application with six biographical questions, a resume, two verbal reasoning questions, and an approximately 7-minute video; followed by a 30-minute interview in July, with final decisions in early August.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: Digital Safety and Security
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for Digital Safety and Security to expand the foundation's ability to support grantee digital safety and security. Civil society organizations face targeted information security threats yet many lack the resources and training to maintain robust digital security. Reporting to the Director, Office of the Vice President for U.S. Programs, the Fellow will identify field-building opportunities in the public-interest digital safety and security field, work directly with program officers across US and global teams to integrate digital security capacity into Ford's technical assistance programs, provide strategic support to high-risk grantees, and assess strategic gaps in the ecosystem for safety and security providers. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years' professional experience with media, technology, advocacy or policy organizations; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; sophisticated knowledge of existing and emerging technologies with a focus on surveillance technologies and organizational digital safety and security; and experience designing, implementing or supporting digital safety and security solutions in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office) with approximately 10 to 20 percent domestic and international travel. Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: AI and Philanthropy
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for AI and Philanthropy, an internally-facing role focused on cross-programmatic learning and advisory support. Reporting to the Director, Technology and Society, the AI Tech Fellow will support Foundation staff in building and adapting internal frameworks for evaluating AI use in ways that prioritize mission and strategy and address ethical, sociotechnical, and operational considerations. Responsibilities include designing and curating hands-on trainings, workshops, and learning sessions to help staff build practical familiarity with AI tools and critically assess their value and efficacy; creating accessible documentation and guides on mission-aligned AI experimentation; curating sociotechnical conversations on how philanthropy and the nonprofit sector are experimenting with AI in ways that respect privacy, safety, and equity; monitoring emerging global AI developments and providing timely briefings to program teams; conducting landscape research on AI initiatives in the philanthropy and social justice sectors; and building relationships with AI researchers, practitioners, grantees and partners working on mission-enabling AI. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years working within public-interest, government, civil society, or commercial technology sectors; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; demonstrated sociotechnical expertise in artificial intelligence and related technologies including machine learning systems and generative AI and their societal implications; and experience designing, implementing or supporting AI applications and tools in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office). Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

It supports women and gender-expansive filmmakers, artists, XR creators, and multidisciplinary storytellers working across XR, MR, VR, AR, AI, and game-based narratives, with no prior XR experience required. item.title: Artist Residency Autumn 2026 (Centre for Multispecies Relations and Future Ecologies, Palermo)
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for Multispecies Relations and Future Ecologies

Location: Altarello di Baida district, Palermo, Sicily, Italy. On-site 62-day residency, 12 October - 12 December 2026. Open to all nationalities, but applicants must be officially residing in a Creative Europe country (excluding Italy).

Award: Total allowance of EUR 1,860 (before tax) per artist, plus a travel budget of EUR 400 per artist (for distances under 4,000 km) with a EUR 400 green-mobility top-up, plus a production budget. Also provided: on-site accommodation in a fully furnished independent house (private bedroom; shared kitchen, living room, two bathrooms; outdoor studio space 100m from the working site), access to recycled/salvaged materials through the Centre's partnerships, a bicycle per resident, and a final public event.

Duration: 62-day residency, 12 October - 12 December 2026, structured in five phases (orientation, observation/fieldwork, proposal and ecological feasibility, creation, and public restitution).

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

The Centre for Multispecies Relations and Future Ecologies invites applications from two artists for a 62-day residency (12 October - 12 December 2026) at its site in the Altarello di Baida district of Palermo, Sicily. The residency is structured around a 1,500 sqm garden at the threshold between the city's urban expansion and the historic rural landscape of the surrounding hills; the site, degraded through previous use (soil compaction and significant biodiversity loss), functions as both studio and subject, and artists participate in an ongoing process of ecological transformation, producing interventions that emerge from extended observation and fieldwork. Applications are welcome across all mediums and disciplines (installation, sound, performance, video, food art, participatory practice, and beyond); research-based and interdisciplinary practices are especially encouraged. The Centre works at the intersection of art, science, and (agro)ecological practices, with a focus on multispecies coexistence and climate change in the Mediterranean, and has been based in Palermo since 2021. PROGRAMME: five phases (orientation with small trips and gatherings; observation with independent fieldwork and consultations with artists, curators, scientists, and anthropologists; proposal and ecological feasibility developed with mentors; creation; and public restitution with an open event, a workshop, and a public discussion). Each artist develops an independent site-specific intervention; the two residents share the site, living space, and process, with exchange encouraged but not prescribed. Artist duos and artists who want to collaborate are encouraged to apply (you can apply as an individual for one position, as a duo for both, or as two artists collaborating for two positions). Mentorship by co-founders/artistic directors Fabio Aranzulla and Luca Cinquemani (weekly site visits, studio discussions, work-in-progress presentations); logistics managed by Francesca Farris. WHO: artists whose work engages with ecology, multispecies relations, or the more-than-human, who can move between observation and making and are interested in dialogue with scientists, land workers, and local communities. Priority is given to artists from working-class and less-privileged backgrounds, those who self-identify as part of marginalised communities, and candidates reflecting diversity in gender, age, geographical background, and ability; green travel is prioritised as a condition of participation where feasible. Applicants should be open to living and working in a neighbourhood on the margins of Palermo's periphery. Funded by Culture Moves Europe (Creative Europe programme of the European Union). APPLICATION: first phase via the online form (opened 10 June); shortlisted artists contacted before the end of July; second phase is video calls with shortlisted candidates; applications accepted in English and Italian. Contact: contact.cmrfe@gmail.com. Deadline 5 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Art Shanty Projects 2027: Call for Performances + Art Actions
item.description:
Organisation: Art Shanty Projects

Location: Bde Uman (Lake Harriet), Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States. On-ice program plus several mandatory in-person meetings in Minneapolis; in-person participation required.

Award: Sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 (request an amount that fits the project: roughly USD 1,000 for full-day/most-ambitious activities, USD 500 for half-day, USD 250 for short or one-off/solo/low-production activities). Plus direct production support, discounted materials and resource sharing, a shared warming shanty, a hot meal, snacks, hot beverages, hand/toe warmers, one parking space per project, on-site medic, and ASL interpreter/audio describer for the public. No application fees.

Duration: On-ice program every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027, with production meetings and mandatory check-ins from September 2026 onward.

Deadline: 09 Jul 2026

Art Shanty Projects seeks winter enthusiasts and creative adventurers to create performances and art actions for its 2027 Program on Bde Uman (Lake Harriet) in Minneapolis. It invites music-makers, poets, builders, scientists, dancers, puppeteers, writers, dreamers, activists, outdoors-people, conversationalists, craftspeople, storytellers, and more to propose new and innovative ways to engage artistically with the public in the social context of a village on a frozen lake (or winter 'beach', as climate change may necessitate). ASP has an expansive notion of what constitutes a performance or art action, having welcomed dance, creative sporting events, happenings, carnival games, music, roving dance, temporary sculpture, puppet shows, artful haircuts, advice booths, climate actions, and more. NOTE: this call is for PERFORMANCES + ART ACTIONS only (a separate call exists for shanty artists). Since 2004, Art Shanty Projects has created joyful winter art experiences inspired by pop-up ice fishing villages, using the frozen lake as a public platform for a temporary creative community with tens of thousands of annual visitors. ASP is deeply committed to social, racial, gender, and economic justice and to accessibility (embracing the spirit of the ADA). ELIGIBILITY: everyone is eligible (artists at all career stages); applicants need not have experience with everything asked, but should show eagerness to engage, develop new skills, and stay open to new perspectives. Projects that involve selling anything or the exchange of legal currency will not be accepted. COMPENSATION: sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 plus extensive production and logistical support. PROGRAM: on-ice every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027; selected artists notified mid-August 2026, with mandatory in-person meetings (at least one team member) starting 10 September 2026. Apply via the Google form (preferred) or the no-Google-account alternative. Contact Erin Lavelle, Artistic Director: program@artshantyprojects.org. Deadline for proposal submissions: 9 July 2026, 11:59pm CST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Justice + Joy Summer Research Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Justice + Joy

Location: United States; fully virtual program. Capstone showcase tentatively in person (mid-September 2026), with travel and lodging covered.

Award: USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful program completion, plus skills-based training, guest speaker sessions, and peer community. All travel and lodging for the in-person capstone are covered.

Duration: Seven-week virtual program, 9 July to 20 August 2026, with a capstone showcase mid-September 2026. Weekly Wednesday sessions 5:00-6:30 PM ET plus approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week.

Deadline: 09 Jul 2026

The Justice + Joy Research Fellowship is a seven-week virtual summer program for girls, young women, and gender-expansive young people of color (ages 18-26) in the United States who want to explore issues impacting their lives, communities, and futures. Fellows identify a topic they care about, such as mental health, education, reproductive justice, immigration, AI and technology, gender-based violence, beauty standards, young parenthood, or another issue affecting their communities, and develop an original research project grounded in their experiences, questions, and ideas. Throughout the summer, fellows participate in weekly virtual sessions, engage with guest speakers, build community with peers from across the country, and receive mentorship and support as they develop their research. The fellowship culminates in a capstone showcase where fellows present their work to the Justice + Joy community, including organizational leaders, partners, and supporters. No prior research experience is required; the program seeks young people who are curious, committed to learning, and interested in using research as a tool for understanding, advocacy, and change. COMMITMENT: weekly virtual sessions on Wednesdays 5:00-6:30 PM ET (9 July - 20 August 2026); approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week; required orientation on Wednesday 9 July; development of an individual research proposal or conceptual framework; presentation at the capstone showcase (tentatively in person, mid-September 2026, with all travel and lodging covered). WHAT YOU GET: a USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful completion; skills-based training in research methods, social justice frameworks, and community advocacy; guest speaker sessions; a peer community; and a real audience for the research (the Justice + Joy board and funders). Apply via the Google Form. Note: an explicit application deadline is not stated; the required orientation and program start is 9 July 2026, so apply before then.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: OpenAI Economic Research Exchange 2026
item.description:
Organisation: OpenAI

Location: Remote / global. Structured, project-based research collaborations with OpenAI Economic Research. No location restriction stated.

Award: USD 25,000 project grant plus a USD 7,500 monthly stipend per selected researcher.

Duration: Carefully scoped projects with defined milestones. OpenAI supports a portfolio across time horizons: short-term projects (2-6 months) using existing approved data, and medium-term studies (6-12 months) that track outcomes over time or test specific interventions.

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

The OpenAI Economic Research Exchange is a new program supporting high-impact external research on the economic effects of AI. Through the Exchange, selected researchers conduct work via structured, project-based collaborations with OpenAI Economic Research, with the goal of producing credible, independent evidence on how AI is affecting workers, firms, institutions, and the broader economy. OpenAI is seeking projects that address important questions about the economic impacts of AI; proposals should explain how carefully governed, privacy-protected use of OpenAI tools and datasets can help answer these questions, extending beyond what traditional datasets alone allow, while maintaining clear safeguards for user privacy and responsible data use. Core questions include labor market effects (moving beyond AI exposure to measure which occupations are likely to see augmentation, displacement, new task creation, or employment growth as model capabilities improve) and measuring economic value (willingness to pay, revealed value, task-level time savings, avoided costs, quality-adjusted output, and durable productivity gains). Selected researchers undertake carefully scoped projects with defined milestones, data governance, and review processes. WHO SHOULD APPLY: researchers with strong empirical skills and relevant expertise in applied causal inference, measurement, labor economics, productivity, firms, education, entrepreneurship, public finance, regional economics, development, inequality, or related fields. EVALUATION: proposals are assessed on methodological rigor, feasibility, fit with Exchange priorities, clear milestones, and the potential to contribute credible external evidence on AI's economic impacts. The Exchange builds on OpenAI's broader measurement efforts, including OpenAI Signals. For more information see the Request for Proposals, or contact econresearch@openai.com. Applications close 5 July 2026; selected researchers will be notified by 31 July 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Windgate Artist-in-Residence Fall 2026 (Arkansas Tech University)
item.description:
Organisation: Arkansas Tech University (supported by the Windgate Charitable Foundation)

Location: Russellville, Arkansas, United States (Russellville campus, in the Arkansas River Valley, about one hour from Little Rock). In-person, full-time residency.

Award: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes) plus USD 18,000 for supplies. Housing provided (a 2-bed, 2-bath campus apartment) and a large, climate-controlled, dedicated off-campus studio space.

Duration: Five-month residency over the Fall academic term 2026.

Deadline: 03 Jul 2026

The Artist-in-Residence (AIR) program at Arkansas Tech University is an immersive experience within an art and educational community, intended to advance the careers of practicing artists, present residents as role models for the ATU community, and strengthen public awareness of the significance of making art and its effect on the surrounding community. The residency takes place over a five-month period, giving the artist time and space to focus on the creative process. For Fall 2026, the program will select an artist to create a public artwork that enhances the ATU campus; both two-dimensional and three-dimensional proposals will be considered. The program is supported by a generous grant from the Windgate Charitable Foundation. The Department of Art at ATU offers programs in Fine Art, Graphic Design, Game and Interactive Media Design, and Art Education, with a facility in Norman Hall featuring a professional gallery, computer lab, lecture hall, and studios for painting, drawing, printmaking, sculpture, ceramics, and graphic design. RESIDENCY DETAILS & EXPECTATIONS: residents must be artists with significant exhibition records, strong communication skills, sustained studio practice, and a desire to engage with the larger campus community; involvement may include guest lecturing, public talks, mentoring students, open studios, and/or collaborating with faculty and students. The resident sometimes teaches one course in 2D Design, 3D Design, or Introduction to Drawing during their stay when enrollment allows. Artists are expected to commit as a full-time resident for the Fall academic term 2026. BENEFITS: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes), USD 18,000 for supplies, a 2-bed/2-bath campus apartment, and a large climate-controlled off-campus studio. Deadline 3 July 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Horizon Europe Cluster 2: Culture, Creativity and Inclusive Society (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE) — incl. AI and Creative Content topic
item.description:
Organisation: European Research Executive Agency (REA) / European Commission, Horizon Europe

Location: European Union and associated countries. Consortium-based; typically requires partners from multiple EU/Horizon member states.

Award: Indicative total budget €85.5 million across 7 topics (2026 call). Individual project amounts vary by topic. Up to 100% funding rate for non-profit research organizations. No application fee.

Duration: Typically 3-4 years; varies by topic.

Deadline: 23 Sep 2026

Horizon Europe Cluster 2 funds research on culture, creativity, and inclusive society. The 2026 call (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE, launched May 12, 2026) covers 7 topics with an indicative budget of €85.5 million. Topic HERITAGE-04 is explicitly titled 'Towards a fair and transparent market for cultural and creative content in the era of generative AI,' covering: impacts of generative AI on creative industries employment; upskilling needs for creative workers; policy and regulatory frameworks; fair remuneration and transparency. Highly relevant for research into AI and cultural/creative industries, artists' rights in the AI era, and sociotechnical studies of AI and culture. ELIGIBILITY: consortium-based applications (minimum 3 organisations from 3 different EU/associated countries are typical for Horizon RIA grants; check call conditions on the Funding & Tenders portal for specific requirements). Deadline: 23 September 2026. Apply via the EU Funding & Tenders portal (ec.europa.eu/info/funding-tenders).

Open call details

item.title: Freelands Foundation: Learning Through Making Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Freelands Foundation

Location: UK (England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, Wales); activity must occur within the United Kingdom.

Award: Up to £25,000. No application fee.

Duration: Projects of any length, taking place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. Funding provided no earlier than December 2026.

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

The Learning Through Making fund supports UK-based visual art organisations to bring opportunities for making and material exploration into their art education work. Freelands Foundation is looking for exploratory projects that embrace openness, collaboration, and iteration -- enabling organisations to work with materials in an expansive way, unpicking preconceptions about making and material literacy without predefined outcomes. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: providing materials, transport, or access to expertise and facilities; action-research trials such as a new series of workshops or events developing material literacy; adding a making-focused element to existing activity. Projects may range from a few days to several months, but must take place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. AMOUNT: up to £25,000; funding provided no earlier than December 2026. ELIGIBILITY: UK-based visual art organisations run with a charitable purpose and for public benefit; eligible types include charities registered in England and Wales, Scotland, or Northern Ireland; exempt charities; galleries, museums, or organisations within a Local Authority or larger charitable body (e.g. universities); and Community Interest Companies or Community Benefit Societies meeting specific criteria (charitable purposes, asset lock, no dividend distribution, ability to segregate restricted funds). Organisations with a prior Freelands Foundation grant relationship are eligible; you may not apply for activity already funded by Freelands Foundation. Individuals are not eligible. HOW TO APPLY: download the Application Form, Application Guidance, and optional Access Form from freelandsfoundation.co.uk; email completed form to grants@freelandsfoundation.co.uk with subject line 'LTM Fund - Application'; one additional page of images or diagrams may be included. APPLICATION SUPPORT SESSIONS (online): 9 July 2026, 10:30am-12pm; 12 August 2026, 10:30am-12pm. KEY DATES: applications open 17 June 2026; close 11 September 2026 at 12pm; outcomes shared 23 November 2026.

Open call details

item.title: AY-ROOP / Le Milieu: Reuse - (Re)making for Circus Residency 2026 (Rennes Métropole, France)
item.description:
Organisation: AY-ROOP

Location: Le Milieu, Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole, France. Applicants must legally reside in a Creative Europe country other than France and the French overseas territories (DROM-COM).

Award: Funded through Culture Moves Europe: EUR 900 mobility allowance per person plus flat-rate travel coverage; supplements for green mobility, visa fees, dependent children under 18, OCT/OR residents, and disability/accessibility needs (including possible full coverage of a sign-language interpreter). On-site single-room accommodation, lunches at Le Milieu canteen (Tuesday to Friday), shared kitchen, and technical support provided. No application fee.

Duration: 21-day residency, 28 November to 18 December 2026.

Deadline: 03 Jul 2026

AY-ROOP is launching an open call to select three circus artists and/or circus set designers/builders for a 21-day Culture Moves Europe research residency at Le Milieu (Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole) from 28 November to 18 December 2026. The residency is a research laboratory on reuse in contemporary circus: how everyday, locally available materials (paper/cardboard, plastics, lightweight metal, textiles, reclaimed wood, bicycle parts, etc.) can be used to create scenographic elements, sets, props and small apparatuses for juggling, acrobatics and balancing. The core principle is that objects are recreated locally from resources available in each territory rather than transported. AY-ROOP provides an equipped construction workshop, a rehearsal stage and circus tent, a guide to local reuse-sector contacts, and a full residency environment (accommodation, shared kitchen, working facilities); two mentors (a reuse specialist and a performing-arts professional) support the artists. PROFILES: circus artists interested in construction/scenography/DIY making, and builders/set designers working in circus; individual or group applications (max 3 people per group); emerging or established, any background. Accessible to artists with disabilities and accessibility needs. Working language English. No aerial apparatus or rigging: all work is ground-based. ELIGIBILITY: aged 18+ (no upper limit); legally resident in a Creative Europe country other than France and DROM-COM; available for the full residency. APPLY with a single PDF (1-page bio/CV, 5-page portfolio or video links, 1-page statement of intent, proof of residence). DEADLINE: 3 July 2026. Results announced 13 July 2026. Check the linked call PDF for full details.

Open call details

item.title: NEW INC Year 13 Mentor Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: NEW INC (New Museum Incubator)

Location: New York, NY, USA; primarily in-person at 250 Hudson St, lower Manhattan. Some hybrid participation possible.

Award: Dedicated Mentors: $500 honorarium. Office Hours Mentors: $300 honorarium. All mentors also receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum. No application fee.

Duration: Year 13 program (September 2026 onward); average 4-8 hours of engagement per week.

Deadline: 08 Jul 2026

NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. YEAR 13 PROGRAM TRACKS mentors may support: Art & Code; Creative Science; Extended Realities; Launchpad; Social Architecture. KEY DATES: Mentor Open Call closes 8 July 2026; Mentor Open House (virtual info session) 17 June 2026, 12-1pm ET (register via the site); mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking ranking session 11 September 2026; mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking event 18 September 2026; Dedicated Mentor matches announced 2 October 2026. Most NEW INC activities take place Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET. Apply at https://www.newmuseum.org/new-inc/open-call/.

Open call details

item.title: KB Researcher-in-Residence (RiR) 2027
item.description:
Organisation: KB, National Library of the Netherlands

Location: The Hague, Netherlands (two days per week on-site at the KB premises)

Award: Secondment at 0.5 FTE for 6 months based on your current salary, up to EUR 5,590 gross per month for a full-time-equivalent position, plus travel expenses, office facilities, and support from a research software engineer, research advisor, and collection and data specialists. No application fee.

Duration: 6 months at 0.5 FTE (two days per week on-site); two positions in 2027, starting Q1 and Q3

Deadline: 27 Sep 2026

ELIGIBILITY: two Researcher-in-Residence positions in 2027. Open to early-career researchers of any discipline (data science, social science, computer science, humanities) who are currently working on a PhD or within 5 years of their PhD end date. You must have, or be able to secure, an affiliation with a Dutch university or research institute willing to second you; external PhD students may use the funding to obtain such a contract. Your project must make use of one or more of the KB digital collections and you must be able to work with primarily Dutch data. You must be available at 0.5 FTE over 6 months and able to spend two days a week on-site at the KB in The Hague. COLLECTIONS: over 130 million digitized pages of newspapers, magazines, radio bulletins and books from 1470 onward (via Delpher and DBNL), a born-digital web collection of more than 23,000 websites, ground-truth and derived datasets on the KB Lab, and metadata including Linked Data. APPLY: use the official proposal template and submit a single PDF by 27 September 2026 to dh@kb.nl, after reading the call's terms and conditions; questions to dh@kb.nl. TIMELINE: deadline 27 September 2026; internal eligibility review followed by an external committee review; outcome notified November 2026; residencies start Q1 and Q3 2027. DISCIPLINES: digital humanities / data science / computational research with cultural-heritage collections. NOTE: the output is usually a dataset or tool prototype hosted on the KB Lab; you agree to share results via blog posts on the KB Lab and to publish resulting tools or data under an open license within 6 months of the residency. Proposals should align with the KB Research Agenda or the Cultural AI Lab.

Open call details

item.title: OIF: Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods 2026 (Francophonie)
item.description:
Organisation: Organisation internationale de la Francophonie (OIF)

Location: Francophone zone (OIF member states globally)

Award: Up to EUR 5,000 for artist mobility (travel, accommodation, visas, logistics). Up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs). Both can be combined. No application fee.

Duration: Residencies or tours of 5 days to 3 months. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026.

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The OIF Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods programme provides grants of up to EUR 5,000 for mobility (travel, accommodation, visa fees, logistics) and up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs) within the Francophone zone; both can be combined. ELIGIBILITY: professional artists and established artistic collectives with a demonstrated connection to the Francophone space (by nationality, working language, or artistic collaborations), residing in an OIF member state, with minimum 2 years of experience in the cultural or artistic sector; must directly manage the project (not act as intermediaries); one application per individual per call. ELIGIBLE DISCIPLINES: live performance (music, theatre, dance, comedy, storytelling), cinema/audiovisual, literature/publishing, and visual arts. PRIORITY given to projects promoting gender equality, women artists, creators under 35, and South-South mobility. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026. Multiple selection sessions throughout the year; call open until 31 August 2026. Information webinars: 11 March, 13 May, and 8 July 2026 at 3 PM Paris time. Contact: culture.dlc@francophonie.org. Apply at https://appelsaprojets.francophonie.org/fr/applications/soutien-a-la-circulation-des-biens-et-mobilite-des-artistes.

Canon Foundation in Europe Research Fellowships support bilateral research exchange between Europe and Japan, awarding up to 15 fellowships annually. item.title: Center for Humane Technology: Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship (Inaugural Cohort)
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Humane Technology (CHT)

Location: Remote, 10 hours/week overlapping with EST required.

Award: $30,000 fixed stipend paid in three milestone-based tranches. No application fee.

Duration: 6 months: September 14, 2026 through March 18, 2027. 15 hours per week.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

CHT's inaugural Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship places 3 fellows (one per focus area) in a 6-month, part-time program to develop rigorous public-impact research and writing on how AI is reshaping society. Fellows choose from 5 focus areas: (1) AI and Cognition — what AI does to human judgment, critical thinking, and intellectual independence; (2) AI and Relationships — AI's effects on intimacy, companionship, and social bonds; (3) AI and Surveillance — how AI enables monitoring, tracking, and control; (4) AI and Spirituality — AI's intersection with meaning, transcendence, and religious practice; (5) Inside the Machine: AI From a Technologist's Perspective. Up to 2 focus areas per application. DELIVERABLES: one flagship research piece, report, or long-form essay for public impact; 1-3 op-eds (placed in target outlets or CHT Substack); one podcast appearance (Your Undivided Attention or equivalent); cohort meetings and bi-weekly check-ins. SUPPORT: bi-weekly guidance from Senior Director of Strategy and Impact; editorial, communications and graphic design support; media training and PR placement support; CHT email address and Slack access; listing on CHT's public fellows page. WHO SHOULD APPLY: mid-career researchers (psychology, sociology, philosophy, cognitive science, communications, STS), policy analysts, journalists covering tech and society, civil society advocates, or technologists with insider perspective. Strong preference for those with a public writing track record (op-eds, Substack, peer-reviewed work) and some public presence. HOW TO APPLY: 1-2 page research proposal per topic (up to 2), CV/resume (2 pages max), 2-minute video explaining fit, writing sample. Deadline: Sunday 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EST.

Open call details

item.title: European Commission: Safeguarding Europe's Born-Digital Heritage (PPPA-2026-BORN-DIGITAL)
item.description:
Organisation: European Commission (Pilot Projects and Preparatory Actions, Culture strand)

Location: European Union. Consortium of at least 5 independent entities from 5 different EU member states required.

Award: Up to €1,985,000, covering up to 85% of eligible project costs (15% co-financing required). No application fee.

Duration: Project duration to be defined in the proposal; covers a scoping and mapping phase across EU member states.

Deadline: 16 Jul 2026

The European Commission is offering a single grant of up to €1,985,000 (85% co-financing) for a project focused on safeguarding born-digital cultural heritage across EU member states. Scope includes: assessing the preservation state of born-digital art, web archives, video games, and digital content created outside cultural institutions; mapping EU and national legal frameworks; identifying non-legislative barriers (technical, financial, organisational, cultural); and providing guidance on creating preservation copies of licensed or legally accessible materials. This is a scoping and advocacy project, not a digitisation grant. ELIGIBILITY: the application must be led by a consortium of at least 5 independent entities from 5 different EU countries, which rules out solo applicants and single-country organisations. Grant reference: PPPA-2026-BORN-DIGITAL. Deadline: 16 July 2026. Verify the current deadline at ec.europa.eu before applying (one secondary source listed 1 June 2026; the primary call-fiche PDF and the Cyprus government funding portal both confirm 16 July 2026).
